ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general chestnut entry; no specific organic or Japanese chestnut variant code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers chestnuts as a category; no organic-specific or Japanese-specific sub-code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 256329004 covers chestnut as a substance; no organic or Japanese chestnut-specific code exists. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is a pack of 2 organic chestnuts, weighing approximately 60 g, as sold at Sushi Express in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Chestnuts, European, raw, unpeeled (NDB No. 12098)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central — SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for raw European chestnut (FDC ID 170578) used as the closest approved analogue for organic chestnut. Japanese chestnut (Castanea crenata) has a very similar macronutrient profile to European chestnut (Castanea sativa). Values are per 100 g edible portion. Organic certification does not materially alter macronutrient composition.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Chestnut, boiled
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 54 (low) is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) for boiled chestnut. Japanese chestnut (Castanea crenata) is botanically and compositionally analogous to European chestnut; GI is expected to be in the same low range. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 27.3) / 100 ≈ 14.7, rounded to 14.8 for a 60 g serving (27.3 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (3 mg per 100 g) and high potassium content (518 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Chestnuts are naturally very low in sodium and rich in potassium, making them a favourable snack choice for individuals managing hypertension; portion size should remain moderate to control total caloric intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (GI 54) and significant carbohydrate content (45.5 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chestnuts have a low-to-moderate GI of 54 and a relatively high carbohydrate load compared to other nuts; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit serving size to 1–2 chestnuts and account for the carbohydrate contribution within their daily meal plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.43 g per 100 g), zero cholesterol, and high dietary fibre (8.1 g per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chestnuts are low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free, with a meaningful dietary fibre content that may support LDL cholesterol management; they are a heart-friendly nut option when consumed in moderate portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Organic Chestnut (有機栗, Yūki Kuri) is a whole or peeled chestnut sourced from organically certified cultivation, served as a snack or ingredient in Japanese cuisine. Chestnuts (Castanea species) are unique among nuts for their high starch and carbohydrate content (approximately 45 g per 100 g) and relatively low fat content (approximately 2.3 g per 100 g), distinguishing them nutritionally from other tree nuts such as almonds or walnuts. They have a naturally sweet, mildly earthy flavour with a soft, starchy texture when boiled or steamed, and a firmer, slightly caramelised texture when roasted. In Japanese culinary tradition, chestnuts (kuri, 栗) are prized seasonal ingredients used in dishes such as kuri gohan (chestnut rice), wagashi (traditional confections), and as standalone snacks. Organically grown chestnuts are free from synthetic pesticides and fertilisers. Nutritionally, chestnuts provide a good source of dietary fibre (8.1 g per 100 g), vitamin C (43 mg per 100 g), potassium (518 mg per 100 g), and folate (62 mcg DFE per 100 g). Their glycemic index is approximately 54 (low category), making them a moderate-GI carbohydrate source. Clinically, chestnuts are suitable for cardiovascular health due to their negligible saturated fat and zero cholesterol content. Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size carefully given the significant carbohydrate load. As sold at Sushi Express in Singapore, organic chestnuts are offered in a pack of 2 (approximately 60 g), priced at SGD 7.00.
common_user_queries: What is the GI of chestnut?
How many calories in a chestnut?
What is kuri in Japanese food?
Is chestnut a nut or a starch?
Can I eat chestnuts if I have high blood pressure?
Are organic chestnuts different nutritionally from regular chestnuts?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for European chestnut (Castanea sativa), which is the closest approved analogue to Japanese chestnut (Castanea crenata). The macronutrient profiles of these two species are very similar. Organic certification does not materially alter nutrient composition. GI is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database via the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables for boiled chestnut. No exact HPB Singapore or ICMR-NIN entry for organic chestnut was available; confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are per 100 g edible portion, adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170578, European chestnut, raw, unpeeled) as the closest approved analogue. Japanese chestnut (Castanea crenata) has a comparable macronutrient profile. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero: chestnuts are a plant food with no industrially produced trans fats; USDA FoodData Central reports 0 g trans fat for chestnuts. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: chestnuts are a plant food and contain no dietary cholesterol per USDA FoodData Central. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: chestnuts are a plant food and contain no vitamin B12 per USDA FoodData Central. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: USDA FoodData Central reports 0 mcg vitamin D for raw chestnuts. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all reported from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for European chestnut. 7. GI of 54 is from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) for boiled chestnut; used as analogous estimate for Japanese organic chestnut. 8. Serving size of 60 g (pack of 2) is estimated based on typical whole chestnut weight of approximately 25–35 g per nut. 9. food_group changed from input 'Mixed' to 'Snacks' as chestnuts are sold as a snack item in this context. 10. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03301710 is used as closest parent for chestnut (whole); no organic-specific or Japanese-specific FoodOn code exists. 11. FoodEx2 code A00LC (Chestnuts) is used as closest parent; no organic sub-code exists in FoodEx2. 12. SNOMED CT 256329004 (Chestnut substance) is used as closest parent.
